content:
Introduction In my previous post AI-Powering Dreams, I documented my experience using Claude Code with glm5 to build a cross-platform skill management tool.
This time, I set my sights on my own blog. With AI assistance, I completed a series of improvements in just 12 hours that I previously found too complex or troublesome to tackle.
Transformation Checklist Here’s what was accomplished:
Theme Migration Switched from my old theme to PaperMod, which offers:
Clean and elegant design that matches my aesthetic Responsive design, mobile-friendly Built-in dark mode Excellent SEO support The migration involved rewriting numerous configuration files:
All files under config/_default/ Homepage, archives, links pages Custom style files Honestly, if I had configured this manually, just researching each configuration option would have taken considerable time. But with the assistance of Claude Code + glm4.7, the entire process was much smoother.
Social Media Integration Added multiple social media icons and links:
WeChat (sidebar QR code) GitHub Bilibili YouTube These icons weren’t taken directly from the theme. I found official SVGs from Tabler Icons myself or had AI help find them, then manually added them to the project. There were some back-and-forth moments—for instance, the WeChat SVG would get corrected and then changed back incorrectly. The inconsistency was frustrating until I finally added this rule directly to CLAUDE.md:
## The WeChat social media SVG is fixed. Do not modify it! Share Button Enhancement Enhanced article sharing functionality to support:
WeChat Moments Zhihu Twitter Facebook LinkedIn Although the Zhihu share button still has a bug, I’m quite satisfied with the current result—I’d give it a 90 out of 100.
Comprehensive SEO Optimization This was something I’d wanted to do for ages but found too complex:
Structured Data:
BlogPosting FAQPage HowTo VideoObject Review SoftwareApplication Verification Configuration:
Completed Google Search Console verification code AI Discoverability:
Added llms.txt file to help AI tools better understand blog content GitHub Actions Performance Optimization My blog deployment uses GitHub Actions, but previous build efficiency was somewhat low.
Optimizations:
Upgraded to Node.js 24 Added Hugo CLI caching Added Git Submodule caching Optimized workflow step structure Build time significantly decreased.
Tag System Refactoring Previously, my blog used both categories and tags, but categories were barely used and their functionality overlapped.
Refactoring:
Removed categories field from all articles Added tags to articles with empty tags Fixed typos (ags → tags) Simplified article creation workflow (Obsidian QuickAdd script optimization) Analytics Integration In addition to existing Google Analytics, added Cloudflare Web Analytics:
Advantages:
Privacy-friendly, no cookies Lightweight, doesn’t affect page load speed Clean, intuitive interface Links Page Redesign Redesigned the friendship links page:
Added social media links (Zhihu, Bilibili, YouTube, GitHub) Added RSS subscription link Optimized link exchange copy Enabled comments (powered by Giscus) Although there are no friendship links yet, I really hope to make more friends and add more links~
How I Worked This transformation was done using Claude Code. The workflow was roughly:
State Requirements: Tell Claude what I want to do Analyze Options: Claude analyzes the codebase and provides suggestions Confirm Execution: After I confirm, Claude directly modifies the code Verify Results: Push code and check the results The entire process didn’t require me to write any code manually. I only needed to:
Be clear about what I want Judge and confirm Claude’s suggestions Verify if results meet expectations Thoughts After Intensive AI Use Dare to Dream, Dare to Act Previously, when I saw cool features on other blogs, I wanted to add them but felt they were too complex:
Theme configuration with tons of parameters I didn’t understand (felt like spending time on decoration when I should focus on content production) SEO structured data that I knew nothing about (after this experience, I at least know what points need to be considered) GitHub Actions optimization, afraid of breaking things (this is crucial—I remember how hard it was to fix broken deployment actions in the past) Now with AI collaboration, these are no longer barriers. Just state your requirements, and AI will help implement them.
Dramatically Reduced Time Costs This transformation took about 12 hours, but actual operation time was only a few hours:
Most time was spent waiting for GitHub Actions deployment Actual thinking and decision-making time was limited If I had researched from scratch:
Researching theme configuration: could take days Learning SEO best practices: could take a week Optimizing GitHub Actions: might require repeated debugging AI Is the Co-Pilot AI won’t replace you, but will make you stronger:
You still need to know what you want You need to judge whether AI’s suggestions are reasonable You need to verify if results meet expectations AI is the co-pilot. You are the captain.
Next Steps I’ve set a flag for myself: publish at least one blog post per week.
With AI’s assistance, I feel confident this flag is sustainable.
Conclusion AI empowers me to dream big and act bold. Tasks that once seemed complex, troublesome, or undesirable can now be completed with minimal effort, thanks to AI collaboration.
This is not just an efficiency improvement—it’s an expansion of possibilities.
AI powers every dream. This is not empty talk.

title: AI-Powering Dreams: Part 2

content:
download a iso in https://immortalwrt.kyarucloud.moe/releases/23.05.4/targets/x86/64/
add new hard disk image Wait for the image to be imported import a new Virtual Machine set vm params vi /etc/config/network set ipaddr to the address of the same network segment as the router and reboot after reboot , login immortalwrt in web browser by http://192.168.5.100 the default user is root, and the default password is password
summary:
1. download a iso in https://immortalwrt.kyarucloud.moe/releases/23.05.4/targets/x86/64/

  2. add new hard disk image

  3. Wait for the image to be imported

  4. import a new Virtual Machine

  1. set vm params

  1. vi /etc/config/network set ipaddr to the address of the same network segment as the router and reboot

  2. after reboot , login immortalwrt in web browser by http://192.168.5.100 the default user is root, and the default password is password
title: install-immortalwrt-in-Synology
